data.title: Malaysia sees alarming spike in youth bankruptcies as debt rises and wages stagnate
data.description:
Fatin Anuar never imagined that managing just two credit cards would leave her battling months of financial anxiety.
The 25-year-old customer service worker in Kuala Lumpur initially saw them as a buffer for day-to-day spending and emergencies. It felt like a smart way to manage cash flow, she said, but the bills began creeping up until the debt felt like “a weight”.
“It kind of snuck up on me. I wasn’t really keeping track, and one day I looked at the statement and thought, ‘Okay, this is getting serious,’” she told This Week in Asia. She has since cleared one card but still owes about 3,000 ringgit (US$730) on the other.
“People say Gen Z spends too much and it’s a bit unfair,” she said. “Yes, we sometimes buy coffee or gadgets here and there, but a lot of spending is just about keeping up with life – rent, food, transport, student loans. But we’re not trying to be careless, we’re just trying to survive.”


Stories like Fatin’s are playing out across Malaysia as a generation of young workers contends with stagnant wages, rising living costs and the widespread availability of easy credit – pressures that are eroding financial safety nets and pushing more young people into debt long before they can find their footing.
Data from employment surveys and salary trackers show fresh graduates typically start work earning between 2,500 ringgit and 3,500 ringgit a month, with those in cities such as Kuala Lumpur edging above 3,000 ringgit.
But wages have not kept up with living costs, leaving many struggling to build savings or manage unexpected expenses, experts noted.
Bankruptcy among Malaysians under 30 has surged nearly 60 per cent compared with the same period last year, according to data tabled in Parliament this week.
Senator Sivaraj Chandran, citing broader figures, told the upper house on Monday that the country recorded 4,875 personal bankruptcy cases in the first nine months of 2025, a 5.7 per cent increase from last year. Personal loans accounted for about 45 per cent of all cases, he said, calling the trend worrying as it shows young people beginning their adulthood with “debt around their necks”.
The roots of the problem run deeper than personal choices, said Doris Liew of the Institute for Democracy and Economic Affairs.
“Young Malaysians are entering a labour market that has been chronically low-paying for decades, with wage levels that simply have not kept pace with inflation,” she told This Week in Asia.
“Fresh graduates are the most vulnerable cohort. They enter the workforce on starting salaries that are fundamentally misaligned with urban living expenses … making it difficult to build savings or manage emergencies.”
That combination of factors has driven many to short-term credit just to cover basic monthly expenses, she said.
One of the fastest-growing avenues for that is buy now, pay later (BNPL) financing, which allows shoppers to split small purchases into a series of instalments paid over weeks or months. The model has gained traction among millions of Malaysians because approvals are quick, screening is minimal and instalments are often marketed as zero-interest.

Peter Yong, founder of the personal finance channel Mr Money TV, said financial literacy gaps are a major factor in how easily young Malaysians fall into short-term debt and underestimate the long-term risks of easy credit.
“In Malaysia, we do not have a formal financial literacy education from school level and a lot of people were given access to credit even at a very young age, especially with buy now, pay later,” he said. “At a young age, they have already been given access to credit even without much financial literacy skill set.”
Social media, he added, only amplifies the pressure to spend.
“People tend to show the brighter side of life … and you always wonder, how come other people are earning as much as me but they can spend so much more? And that makes you think, maybe you can do the same.”
As of end-September, Malaysian households held 54.9 billion ringgit in combined credit card and BNPL debt, Deputy Finance Minister Lim Hui Ying told Parliament last week.
Credit card balances made up more than 90 per cent of the total, while BNPL accounts carried 4.2 billion ringgit in outstanding payments across seven million accounts, with overdue payments at 3.5 per cent.
New rules under the Consumer Credit Act, expected to take effect by year-end, will require affordability checks, clearer fee structures and ethical collection practices for BNPL and other non-bank lenders.
Such reforms cannot come soon enough for consumers like Fatin, who said she is still learning to navigate her finances even as her budget remains tight.
“Sometimes it feels like you’re always choosing between being responsible and actually enjoying life a bit,” she said.

data.title: Indonesia closes door on Airbnb ban after Bali governor seeks to curb unlicensed rentals
data.description:
Indonesia has confirmed it will not ban holiday rental platform Airbnb after Bali said it was considering doing so to curb unlicensed hotels and villas that have mushroomed on the island.
The split underscores the growing tension between Bali’s push to rein in illegal accommodations and Jakarta’s preference for regulation over platform bans. Analysts also say that a ban on Airbnb alone would not fix deeper problems such as unregistered guest houses, weak enforcement and foreigners subletting properties.
Indonesia’s Ministry of Tourism sought to clarify the issue after Bali Governor I Wayan Koster publicly declared he would seek to ban the rental platform, stressing that the government “has never banned and does not intend to halt” the operations of online travel agencies, including the platform.
“The government is currently undertaking the structuring of illegal tourism accommodations. This measure is not a restriction on [online] platforms. The policy is part of the government’s broader effort to enhance service quality, ensure visitor safety and promote fair business competition,” the ministry said in a statement shared with This Week in Asia on Tuesday.

Koster had announced plans last week to pursue a ban on Airbnb, arguing it was necessary because unlicensed accommodations were undermining Bali’s economy. He said the more than 2,000 unlicensed hotels in Bali contributed nothing to local revenue.
The Indonesian Hotel and Restaurant Association (PHRI) in Bali estimates there are 16,000 villas, guest houses and hotels on the island, but only 370 are licensed.
Many foreigners rented locally owned properties long term then put them back on the market via Airbnb as daily rentals, Koster said. They could charge tourists very low prices as they did not need to pay taxes.
“It’s a shame for licensed hotels, which have to pay taxes and compete with [illegal] lodgings. We already have the data. We will propose a ban [on Airbnb], and action will be taken starting in 2026,” Koster said on December 3, as cited by news outlet Kompas.
“The number of tourists has increased, but the occupancy rate hasn’t kept pace with the number of visitors to Bali. Furthermore, this is also hurting regional revenue. They do not fully support the Balinese economy.”
Koster said he would “crack down on all of them”, but urged industry players as well as regents and mayors on the island “to be united, as it’s not just the government’s responsibility, but all of ours”.
Beyond Airbnb
While Koster blamed online services such as Airbnb for enabling the marketing of unlicensed lodgings, tourism experts such as I Made Suniastha Amerta said that the problem on the ground was “more complex”.
“Bali is experiencing overtourism, meaning that the number of tourists coming is very high, but it does not necessarily increase the significant benefits for licensed accommodations,” Suniastha Amerta, a lecturer of tourism and management at Warmadewa University in Bali’s capital Denpasar, told This Week in Asia.
“However, this is not the platform’s fault. Many locals own small guest houses but don’t register to avoid paying taxes. They sell their accommodations online, either through Airbnb or other similar platforms.”
There were also many foreigners who rented local-owned villas to rent out to tourists because “they have mastered digital marketing”, Suniastha Amerta said.


These unlicensed lodgings had been interfering with proper policymaking in Bali as the local government lacked complete data on tourist visits, local revenue and occupancy rates, Tjokorda Oka Artha Ardana Sukawati, head of PHRI Bali, said on December 3, as cited by news outlet Detik.
Ida Bagus Raka Suardana, a lecturer in economics at the National Education University in Denpasar, said that weak law enforcement was also contributing to the rise in illegal lodgings.
“Lower-level officials, such as traditional village and neighbourhood heads, often recognise these accommodations as residential homes,” Raka Suardana said. “These officials should know that these houses are lodgings for tourists. The question is whether they’re profiting from them. That’s the biggest problem, especially in southern Bali.”
Trying to ban Airbnb in a particular location within a country would not be easy.
“To block an application or website, it cannot be done by just any party. In Indonesia, it is carried out by the Indonesian Internet Service Providers Association, but they can only block something on the orders of the Ministry of Communication and Digital,” said Heru Sutadi, executive director of the Indonesia Information and Communication Technology Institute.
The Bali government can propose a ban of Airbnb to the ministry, but it may be met with refusal from other regions that still welcome the platform, according to Heru.
“The ministry could coordinate with Airbnb to not open its services for the Bali region, so all properties with addresses in Bali will not be found on Airbnb. However, banning Airbnb for only one region, in practice, will be difficult,” Heru said.
Platform for change
The Ministry of Tourism said that it had been tightening supervision of lodging operators in Bali, Yogyakarta, West Nusa Tenggara and West Java since March.
It also works with online platforms, which it sees as “an integral part of Indonesia’s digital tourism ecosystem,” to enforce licensing and registration on all accommodations.
“Business legality is not merely an administrative formality. [Licensing] is a prerequisite for ensuring that a tourism accommodation meets safety standards, professional requirements and fiscal obligations that directly contribute to regional own-source revenue and national tax collection,” the ministry said.

Any hotels, villas or guest houses that fail to obtain licenses by March 31 next year would be delisted from online rental platforms, the ministry said.
Bali is not the only one grappling with issues brought by short-term rentals, such as housing shortages, rising rents for the locals and impact of mass tourism on the environment and communities.
Countries like Britain, Italy, France, Germany, Spain, and the Netherlands have imposed stricter rules such as night caps, a ban on key boxes on public land and tightening zoning regulations.

data.title: As more department stores in Singapore struggle, can they adapt and survive?
data.description:
Retired regional human resources director Helen Kuan typically spends this time of year picking up Christmas presents from her usual department store haunts around Singapore. But her options are becoming more limited as more of these one-stop stores have been shuttering in recent years.
On Monday, shopping centre NEX, located in the northeast of Singapore, announced that Japanese brand Isetan would close in April next year, a month after the chain shut its Tampines outlet. With these closures, Isetan’s Scotts Road store and its leased-out space at Wisma Atria are what the department store has left in the city state, where it once had six outlets.
Retail experts told This Week in Asia that department stores worldwide are under threat from changing consumer patterns and e-commerce. For Kuan, 63, the main reason to keep returning to the stores is the shopping experience.
“Maybe as part of the older generation, I like the social interaction. I like to see and touch, and get the salesperson to tell me about the products and walk out with what I want straight away, while knowing exactly what I’m going to get. [Shopping] online feels colder, and I’ve been scammed by lousy products,” Kuan told This Week in Asia.
A spokesman for Isetan, which first set up shop in Singapore in the 1970s, said in response to media queries that it was closing because of the “expiry of its lease agreement and as part of a strategic review of business operations”. The brand was looking to realign resources and strengthen its focus on its flagship Isetan Scotts store in Orchard.
“Isetan Scotts continues to be our core retail focus in Singapore, and we have invested in upgrades, refreshed concepts and new tenants to strengthen the overall shopping experience,” the spokesman said.
State-owned broadcaster CNA reported on Thursday that among five major shopping centre operators in the country – Takashimaya Singapore, C K Tang, which operates Tangs, Isetan Singapore, BHG Singapore and Metro Holdings – only Takashimaya and Tangs, both of which own or control their retail space, were profitable.
Takashimaya Singapore reported a net profit of S$43.18 million (US$33 million) for the year ended December 2024, slightly down from 2023 but far outperforming its competitors. C K Tang remained in the black with a S$1.25 million net profit in the 2024 financial year, a decline from S$4.61 million the previous year, according to CNA.

Department stores once thrived in Singapore from the 1960s to the 1990s, with local and international brands such as Robinsons, John Little, Daimaru, Yaohan, SOGO and Oriental Emporium being household names in the city state.
But with more shopping malls having similar offerings under one roof and the proliferation of cheap e-commerce platforms, retail business models based on department stores are increasingly under threat, according to experts.
Yaohan closed in 1997 after its parent company in Japan declared bankruptcy, while Daimaru shut down in 2003. In 2005, Seiyu sold its Singapore business to CapitaLand, which in turn sold it to Beijing Hualian Group.
John Little closed its last outlet in 2016, marking the end of Singapore’s oldest department store, which opened in 1842. Robinsons closed its last physical stores in Singapore in The Heeren and Raffles City in December 2020, after 162 years of operations.
Lynda Wee, adjunct associate professor at Nanyang Business School in Nanyang Technological University, told This Week in Asia it was likely that conventional department stores with leased department concepts would follow suit, especially those that continue to rely on old formats, large footfall and broad but undifferentiated assortments.
“Retail is evolving towards specialisation, storytelling and differentiation, and the future will be shaped by brands that elevate their product quality, brand experience and emotional relevance. Department stores that fail to adapt to this shift will naturally fade, while those that transform may still find a meaningful though more focused role in the market, starting with their current customer base,” Wee said.
Experts note that while the department store format has been losing steam across the developed world, it is exacerbated in Singapore due to high rental costs, labour costs and a small domestic market.

Seshan Ramaswami, associate professor of marketing education at the Singapore Management University, pointed out that there was also difficulty in hiring retail staff for positions requiring them to be on their feet all day and offering tailored service to local and foreign customers.
Asked if the closure of department stores in recent years could affect Singapore’s reputation as a shopping destination, Ramaswanmi said that its status as a shopping destination for tourists from its biggest markets, such as Indonesia, China and India, had declined in the last decade for multiple reasons.
The biggest cause was the rapid development of modern retail concepts in these countries, providing consumers with a much wider assortment of products and brands domestically at far better prices, he added.
Sulian Tan-Wijaya, executive director of Retail & Lifestyle Savills, noted that outside Singapore, brands such as Selfridges and Harrods in London and Mitsukoshi in Tokyo had evolved successfully with the times.
All three have a strong line-up of luxury brands, proper curation of mid-range fashion brands, vibrant beauty hall and popular food & beverage concepts featuring worldwide cuisine, according to Tan-Wijaya.
“Selfridges is especially popular with the younger, trendy shoppers as they are strong in streetwear and have a well-curated playlist of music that is anything but mainstream. Mitsukoshi also has a roof garden for tired shoppers to rest in between their buying sprees,” she said.

Adapt to survive
In the case of Singapore, Tan-Wijaya argued that younger shoppers preferred individualistic specialty stores over mainstream brands, and bringing these concepts into department stores might help them stay relevant.
“Outdated department stores risk alienating the very important Gen Y and Z demographics and losing relevance very quickly. To appeal to younger shoppers, department stores should introduce more relevant and edgy fashion brands of various price points, newer and cult beauty brands, which you can find in places like Sephora instead of the usual mainstream department store brands,” she said.
Ramaswami said department stores in Singapore should target young shoppers through integrated omnichannel experiences, allowing shoppers to buy online and pick up at a store, or order from a store and have their purchases delivered to them.
They could also tailor shopping experiences to trends, like the Pokemon phenomenon or sporting events, or focus on uniquely Singaporean brands and products to attract tourists.
“Otherwise, once the current loyal middle-aged Singaporean customers retire, that may just signal the beginning of the end for this format in Singapore,” he said.
Kuan, who belongs to the segment of retiree customers, has noticed that the department stores she frequents are attracting far fewer shoppers than in their heyday, and they tend to be older.
“When you look at malls and the department stores in them, it seems like closure is not a matter of if but when.”

data.title: Dish in Focus: Flower crab dan dan noodles at Path
data.description:
Path has emerged as an intimate eight-seater chef’s table in Tsim Sha Tsui, led by rising star chef de cuisine Tony Mok.
Mok draws on his Cantonese roots and experience in Michelin-starred Western kitchens to create a refined but relaxed tasting menu that tells a story with each course. Rather than sticking strictly to tradition, Path treats classic Chinese dishes as a starting point, layering contemporary techniques and international influences while preserving familiar flavours, so that plates such as crab dan dan noodles, salted-egg choux or wide rice noodles with threadfin feel both comforting and quietly surprising.

The green-accented space, built around a single counter repurposed from the previous tenant, underscores Path’s ethos of renewal and honesty, inviting direct interaction between guests and the kitchen for an experience that feels more like being hosted at a chef’s personal table than a conventional fine dining room.
The flower crab dan dan noodles are a signature on the menu – a cold, mul-naengmyeon-style twist on the classic that swaps rich peanut paste for a lighter cashew sauce. “We drew inspiration from classic dan dan noodles and Korean cold soy broth noodles kongguksu, blending cultures with a refreshing twist,” Mok explains.

It fits Path’s modern Chinese ethos by challenging expectations – noodles over bread – while delivering Hong Kong flair through typhoon shelter elements. Fresh flower crab, sourced daily live from local waters, is extracted whole for a roe-infused dashi that soaks toasted cashews over two to three days to create a velvety base. Mok then blends these with house-made chilli oil. Japanese somen noodles get an ice bath and are hand-twirled for perfect starch and texture, akin to sushi shaping, then the dish is topped with crispy garlic, breadcrumbs and chilli flakes.
Born from a pop-up with sommelier Wallace Lo a year ago, and refined through private tastings, the dish overcame several hurdles with sauce consistency before landing on the current iteration where the noodles are coated evenly. “We love the elegance of the cashew sauce paired with the delicate flower crabmeat … the typhoon shelter mix adds layers of texture and aroma that elevate the dish and bring Hong Kong flavours into the dish,” Mok shares.

Diners rave about the signature – some even booking specifically for this dish and then requesting seconds – which cemented it as a menu anchor. “Imagine a creamy, toasty base with a subtle chilli kick and a velvety texture, all topped with the aromatic crunch of a garlic and breadcrumb crumble,” Mok says, describing a bite.
The counter’s lively intimacy, with its welcoming green tones and communal vibe, amplifies the dish’s shareable appeal amid Path’s story-driven plates like XO broccoli or salted-egg choux.
Ultimately, the flower crab dan dan noodles embody Path as a whole: “Noodles offered a chance to challenge expectations while staying true to our modern Chinese cuisine concept.”

Anatomy of a Dish
Name: Flower crab dan dan noodles
Restaurant: Path
Main ingredients: Fresh flower crab, Japanese somen noodles, toasted cashews, house-made chilli oil, typhoon shelter mix, crispy garlic, breadcrumbs and chilli flakes.
Presentation: Noodles are hand-twirled tableside after an ice bath for refined texture and topped with garlic, chilli and a sprinkle of breadcrumbs.
Flavours: The flower crab dan dan noodles deliver a layered, slow-building richness that feels both comforting and surprisingly light. The first impression is creamy and toasty rather than overtly spicy, with a subtle nuttiness that recalls classic dan dan flavours without the heaviness of peanut or sesame.

data.title: Swift pilots blockchain payments with Ant International and HSBC in cross-border test
data.description:
Ant International, HSBC and Swift have successfully tested cross-border transfers of tokenised deposits using an international financial messaging standard, in a move that could streamline global blockchain-based payments and corporate treasury management.
The milestone, announced on Thursday, marked the first case leveraging the Swift network and ISO 20022 as a messaging standard in cross-border payments using tokenised deposits.
The standard is a universal language for exchanging electronic data in financial business transactions.
In the test, Singapore-based Ant International and HSBC connected Ant’s blockchain infrastructure to Swift’s network, allowing real-time cross-border treasury management between HSBC Singapore and Hong Kong through the bank’s tokenised deposit services.
Meanwhile, Swift and HSBC introduced a common protocol that removed the need for Ant International to establish individual bilateral arrangements with banks.
“[This] ISO 20022-enabled solution allows blockchain interoperability on Swift’s network, using Ant International’s technology and HSBC’s Tokenised Deposit Service,” the three firms said in a joint statement.

The initiative would allow digital money issuers to use the financial messaging standard and Swift’s network to simplify adoption by banks and external clients, instead of building separate connections with each institution, while enhancing interoperability between digital and traditional fiat money.
Swift’s messaging platform, products and services connect more than 11,500 banking and securities organisations, market infrastructures and corporate customers in more than 200 countries and territories.
“This integration not only speeds up payment processing but also enhances [anti-money-laundering] and sanctions screening,” said Shirish Wadivkar, global head of payments and cash management at Swift.
“We are excited to demonstrate how ISO 20022 data formats, when combined with new technologies like blockchain, bring significant value to the entire community.”
Lewis Sun, global head of domestic payments and emerging payments at HSBC, said the partnership with established payment rails and emerging blockchain networks was critical as the bank expanded its tokenisation services.
“By enabling tokenised deposits to move securely and efficiently across borders, we are giving our corporate clients more choice in how they manage liquidity globally,” Sun said.
Last year, Ant International became a member of Swift and later joined its programme that aims to enhance transparency in cross-border payments by testing end-to-end transaction tracking across banks, payment service providers and e-wallets.
“We hope our collaboration can guide the standardisation of tokenised deposits under ISO 20022,” said Kelvin Li, general manager of platform tech at Ant International.
Ant International, a spin-off of fintech company Ant Group and an affiliate of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the Post, has been working with a number of banks on its tokenisation initiatives.
Last month, it unveiled work with UBS’ blockchain payments platform to step up its global treasury operations. The firm is also involved in the Hong Kong central bank-led digital asset project.
“We will continue to enable interoperability in global money movement together with our partners, so businesses can access more transparent, secure and efficient cross-border payments,” said Li.

data.title: China husband seeks help after late wife’s virtual house ‘legacy’ in video game was torn down
data.description:
A Chinese man has sought help from a mobile game to restore the virtual house his late wife built so that he can continue remembering her, moving many people online.
The Beijing man posted under the handle @chuwengeileyan on November 26 that the virtual houses he and his wife built in the game ShanHai suddenly disappeared.
ShanHai is an open-world survival crafting game based on the ancient Chinese mythology Shanhai Jing, translated in English as “The Classic of Mountains and Seas”.

A user can either choose battle mode or build their peaceful, idyllic world in the game.
The man and his wife met in 2019 and married a year later. They began playing the game together during the Covid 19 pandemic, while stranded at home.
They spent a lot of time building their world in the game.
It is common for couples to build bonds in games. Some also find dates in games.
His wife once remarked that they chose a great spot in the game, having enough space to build their houses next to each other and own their own virtual farmland.
She was pregnant with their baby in 2024 and was diagnosed with acute leukaemia while eight months pregnant. She had to go into labour early.

After the third chemotherapy, her condition worsened and she was admitted to hospital.
They sold their flat to support her treatment.
However, her life could not be saved. In April this year, she died from a sudden cerebral haemorrhage.
He said their virtual houses in ShanHai became her only legacy he could remember her by, especially after selling their real life flat.
While working hard to raise their child, the man kept the habit of cleaning up their virtual houses every week.
He posted that he discovered a week ago that both their houses had disappeared suddenly.
Some speculated that their houses might be dismantled by other users, which is possible according to the game settings.
He tried to contact the game’s customer service department but was not answered.

He managed to get his house back but was unable to retrieve hers as he could not log into her account, which requires face recognition.
He then asked for help online.
His post reportedly attracted 10,000 likes and the attention of the game’s developers.
On November 29, good news arrived that the developers had successfully restored his wife’s account and her virtual house.
“He did not just cherish the virtual house but also the time and love his wife spent on it,” said one online observer.
“It is a sad reality that our virtual properties are fragile and can vanish very easily. Maybe we should make our peace with this reality,” said another.
“The game should now just make their houses permanent,” said a third.

data.title: Meet Formula One champion Lando Norris’ girlfriend, Portuguese model Margarida Corceiro
data.description:
After beating out Max Verstappen and Oscar Piastri to claim the Formula One championship title at the Abu Dhabi Grand Prix on December 7, Lando Norris ran straight to his girlfriend Margarida Corceiro who was watching from the McLaren garage. The couple shared a sweet victory kiss as Norris embraced his 23-year-old model girlfriend before taking to the podium to celebrate his win.

To fans of F1, Corceiro is a familiar face. The model has been in an on-again, off-again relationship with 26-year-old Norris since 2023.
Here’s everything to know about Margarida Corceiro.
Margarida Corceiro is a model and actress

Margarida Corceiro, better known as Magui to her fans, is a Portuguese model and actress who was born in October 2002. She made her acting debut in the Portuguese telenovela Prisioneira and has since appeared in a number of TV series.
In 2020, Corceiro participated in the Portuguese version of Dancing with the Stars and was featured on Forbes Portugal’s 30 Under 30 list for 2025. She has over 2 million followers on Instagram at the time of writing, with Norris and Glen Powell being among her followers. Corceiro has also partnered with brands like Intimissimi and Alo as a model.
She is an entrepreneur

Aside from her acting career, Corceiro also co-owns the swimwear brand Missus. On its website, the company is described as a brand “for girls who make their own sunshine” with collections “sustainably crafted from recycled ocean plastic”.
How did she meet Lando Norris?

Corceiro was first rumoured to be dating Norris when the pair was spotted spending time together in 2023, but they reportedly split in 2024, as per JustJared.
“Lando and Margarida had a fun time together and hung out a lot,” a source told The Sun. “But it wasn’t working out so they cooled off. He’s free and single and just wants to focus on his sport.”
Given their open display of affection at the Abu Dhabi Grand Prix, it’s safe to say the pair has since reconciled.
Before Norris, Corceiro was known to be dating Chelsea footballer João Félix from 2019 to 2023.

data.title: How Hong Kong’s famous Christmas decorations came to life, as told by one of their creators
data.description:
It was December 1990 and I was four years old. Hong Kong then got much colder than it does today, but standing to my mother’s right as we took in the Christmas lights, my younger brother to her left, the cold seemed to melt away. Wide-eyed and clutching my mother’s hand, I gazed upon the illuminated towers and parapets of a snow-white castle. Angels perched on the battlements, haloes aglow. Fairy lights festooned the winter scene on the waterfront by Harbour City, in Tsim Sha Tsui.
Hong Kong’s ascent was written in Christmas lights. Mirroring the emergence of a booming economy and a burgeoning middle class, the annual citywide illuminations have marked a tradition for many families in the former British colony since the 1950s. Our own family’s ritual had begun in the 60s, when my mother, Clara Chung Lo-ling, was brought by her father to see the lights in Central. Her voice still trails off in a reverie when she recalls the excitement of the occasion. “Everyone was happy and turned out in their best clothes,” she says. “It was beautiful.”

My mother carried these memories and traditions with her, growing up alongside Hong Kong as it flourished from a manufacturing hub to a financial powerhouse. She herself would become part of that meteoric rise, joining the property sector just as Victorian buildings were giving way to the first skyscrapers.
In Hong Kong, traditions are often intertwined with commerce, and the property sector has always been behind the Christmas lights that shine so vividly in the city’s consciousness. While the displays would capture the attention of the city, those responsible for designing and building them often operated unseen by the public.

Among the most prolific of these lighting designers was Ko Cham-yuk. Born in 1951, Ko remembers observing the installation of elaborately hand-painted cinema billboards as a child. Until the mid-90s, these were the norm in Hong Kong, the deft brushstrokes of talented tradesmen capturing the magic in an actor’s eyes and freeze-framing the high-flying kicks of kung fu heroes – works of art befitting the glamour of a trip to the movies. Ko, spellbound, set his heart on joining those artists.
At the time, Ko was working as a washing machine and air-conditioning repairman, but began his set design career in earnest in the late 60s with an apprenticeship at Peacock, one of only a few companies that did such work. He toiled under the gaze of his master, Lau Wai-tong, who is still well known today for having designed the iconic poster for the Hong Kong release of Bruce Lee’s 1973 film Enter the Dragon.

Throughout the 60s and 70s, Central formed the natural heart of Hong Kong’s Christmas celebrations, but, in 1981, revellers who had come to admire the lights on Chater Road turned violent when a car hit a pedestrian on the packed thoroughfare. The ensuing Christmas riots saw vehicles overturned and set ablaze, prompting authorities to curtail large-scale Christmas displays in the city centre.
Nevertheless, it was around this time that “Hong Kong’s economy really started taking off”, recalls Ko. With government backing, the newly reclaimed land of East Tsim Sha Tsui became the epicentre of Christmas cheer, and Sino Land, the developer most associated with the master-planned district, reaped most of the benefit.

Ko’s first Christmas display appeared in the early 80s at Harbour City. It was one of Hong Kong’s first moving displays – if not the first – and featured rows of polyfoam angels holding hymnals and singing, bedecked with lights and moving left and right. “It was very simple for me,” says Ko. “I told [Harbour City] that I could do it, no problem. I threw it in almost as a freebie. After I did that the first time, moving installations became the standard.”
In this first display, Ko brought his early experiences in electronics to bear. “It’s the exact same arrangement that allows a washing machine to function in stages,” he says. “First it lets in the water, then it spins. One switch closes and another opens. That’s how I understood it.”

Ko moved on from that experience to creating displays that grew more exuberant with each passing year. Still, the one that stands out the most to Ko was a suspended design between Miramar Shopping Centre and Miramar Tower on Carnarvon Road in Tsim Sha Tsui. Installation called for a large metal frame with an intricate arrangement of six one-inch-thick cables anchored between it and the pedestrian walkway beneath. On this precarious assemblage, Ko’s team built a bamboo scaffold and worked for an entire week.
“It had never been done before,” says Ko of the technical complexity. “After removing the scaffolding, the lights, fixed to a wire mesh, looked like they were floating in mid-air.”

Each year, Ko would only have the capacity for one or two Christmas projects. “It would have been impossible to do more,” he says. “You would do one for Harbour City and another for Sun Hung Kai and you’d already be pressed for time, as these displays needed to be ready simultaneously.” In the busiest of seasons, he would have to complete entire displays in a single night.
Ko, who retired in 2000 and now lives in San Francisco, in the United States, still looks back with fondness on his early production work, which coincided with the heyday of Hong Kong’s Christmas decorations, when they could draw crowds of up to half a million. The tradition may have faded among a glut of modern entertainment alternatives, but in the aftermath of the isolating years of the Covid-19 pandemic, there is a new appreciation for the warmth and togetherness that the lights evoke.

Today, an enormous tree towers over Central’s Statue Square, lights spiralling around it in a technicolour helix. Beside it sits a row of idyllic cottages, their blueprints seemingly plucked from a chocolate box, while a stately station clock has sprouted behind the statue of Sir Thomas Jackson – a tableau designed for smartphones but still tethered to the hand-painted tungsten bulbs and polyfoam angels of Christmases past.

data.title: Hong Kong’s M+ museum to showcase Zao Wou-Ki’s mastery in printmaking
data.description:
The abstract imagery and expressive colours of the late Chinese-French artist Zao Wou-Ki take centre stage in Hong Kong, as the M+ museum presents Asia’s first and largest retrospective of his print works – including lithographs and intaglios – opening on Saturday.
The show, titled “Zao Wou-Ki: Master Printmaker”, will showcase nearly 180 works that the museum has amassed from two separate donations by the late artist’s wife, Françoise Marquet-Zao, and daughter, Sin-May Roy Zao.
Zao is regarded as one of the most influential abstract artists of the mid to late 20th century. One of his works “Juin-Octobre 1985” was auctioned at HK$510.4 million (US$65 million) in 2018 by Sotheby’s in Hong Kong – a record in the city for an Asian artist.
His works, particularly his oil paintings, remaining staples of the auction market.
Zao, together with Wu Guanzhong and Chu Teh-chun, were dubbed the “Three Musketeers” of Chinese modern art for their achievement in bridging Eastern and Western aesthetics.
His collection at M+, alongside Wu’s extensive portfolio donated to the Hong Kong Museum of Art since 2018, makes Hong Kong unique in housing the largest number of works by two of the three masters.
“This kind of material really shows you a very personal, very intimate aspect of this modern master, which is also one of the goals of the exhibition, to really reveal a lesser-known aspect of this already very well-known master,” Dr Wu Mo, Sigg curator of the museum, said.
Born in Beijing to a wealthy family in 1920, Zao later moved with his family to Shanghai, from where he left for Paris in 1948. He eventually became a naturalised French citizen. Having studied oil painting in China, he began working with lithography in 1949 and continued creating until his death in 2013.

Wu explained that in executing works through a primarily Western medium, Zao infused his prints with his mainland Chinese roots – from drawing inspiration from oracle bone characters dating back over 3,000 years to using traditional Chinese xuan paper.
She highlighted that printmaking was a labour-intensive process, requiring Zao to dedicate one to two months each year working with a team in a studio, often referencing other works he had already created, such as oil paintings and watercolours.
A collection of three works produced between 1970 and 1971 exemplifies this approach, showcasing Zao’s experiments with the same composition across different media. The tonal gradations were recreated in the print from earlier works in oil and watercolour.
The three works are the etching and aquatint work Untitled, watercolour piece Untitled and the oil painting 10.03.78.
“Starting from 1958, [Zao] no longer titled any of his works by descriptive titles because he wanted the viewers to focus more on the imagery itself instead of the narration of it,” Wu explained.
The exhibition also highlights Zao’s interactions and friendships within French artistic circles, including collaborations with poets and commissioned works for the Olympic Games. Curators have also dedicated an area to illustrate the printmaking process using the artist’s own tools.
Tickets are priced at HK$190 for adults, with concessionary tickets at HK$100, which also grant access to other parts of the museum. The exhibition will run until May 3 next year.

data.title: China stresses domestic demand after closely watched economic conference
data.description:
China has pledged to bolster its economy, primarily on the back of domestic demand, and build a robust domestic market to counter external challenges, after a high-level meeting setting the tone for next year’s economic policy.
Following the central economic work conference – an annual gathering of the Central Committee of the ruling Communist Party and the State Council, China’s cabinet – a readout by state news agency Xinhua said the country must “strengthen our own capabilities to cope with external challenges”.
The two-day conference, which typically lays out the broad fiscal and monetary agenda for the coming year, ended on Thursday.
More to follow …
